laws.title: California Minimum Wage — $16.90/hr (Effective January 1, 2026)
laws.summary: California's state minimum wage increased to $16.90/hr effective January 1, 2026 — a $0.40/hr increase from the 2025 rate of $16.50/hr, adjusted for CPI. Note: certain fast food workers are already at $20/hr under AB 1228 (effective April 2024), and healthcare workers have separate sector-specific minimums. Many local jurisdictions (Los Angeles, San Francisco, etc.) have higher local rates.
compliance_actions: Update payroll to $16.90/hr effective January 1, 2026
Check applicable local minimums — many CA cities exceed the state rate
Fast food employers: confirm compliance with the $20/hr sector minimum
Update all required wage notices and postings

laws.title: California PAGA Reform (SB 92) — Revised Private Attorneys General Act
laws.summary: Significant PAGA reforms took effect July 1, 2024 under SB 92. Key changes: employees must now suffer the alleged violation personally to have standing; cure provisions were expanded for certain violations; penalties are reduced for employers with compliant policies; and a new early evaluation conference process was introduced. The reforms provide employers a meaningful opportunity to limit PAGA exposure through proactive compliance.
compliance_actions: Audit pay practices and policies to identify and cure any PAGA exposure
Implement the enhanced cure procedures for technical violations before litigation
Review and update your wage statements, rest/meal break policies, and expense reimbursements
Work with employment counsel on a PAGA audit given the new standing requirements

laws.title: California Workplace Violence Prevention Plan (SB 553) — Required for All Employers
laws.summary: California SB 553 requires virtually all employers (with limited exceptions) to establish, implement, and maintain a written Workplace Violence Prevention Plan (WVPP) — similar to an IIPP. The plan must include hazard identification, employee training, and an incident log. Cal/OSHA will begin enforcement. Employers in healthcare have a separate, longer-standing workplace violence standard.
compliance_actions: Draft or update your Workplace Violence Prevention Plan (WVPP)
Train employees annually on the WVPP and how to report incidents
Maintain a violent incident log for 5 years
Designate a person responsible for implementing the WVPP
Conduct a workplace violence hazard assessment

laws.title: California Pay Transparency — Salary Ranges Required in Job Postings (SB 1162)
laws.summary: California SB 1162 requires all employers with 15+ employees to include pay scale ranges in all job postings (including third-party postings). Employers of any size must provide pay scale information to current employees upon request. Additionally, employers with 100+ employees must submit annual pay data reports to the CRD — including median/mean hourly rates by race/ethnicity/gender within each job category.
laws.employee_threshold_note: Full posting requirements apply to employers with 15+ employees
compliance_actions: Include pay scale ranges in all job postings (15+ employees)
Provide pay scale to any employee who requests it
Submit annual pay data report to CRD if you have 100+ employees
Train recruiters and hiring managers on the new transparency requirements

laws.title: California AI in Employment — Pending Regulation (SB 1047 / DFEH Guidance)
laws.summary: California is actively exploring regulations on the use of artificial intelligence in employment decisions (hiring, performance evaluation, termination). While the major AI safety bill SB 1047 was vetoed in 2024, the Civil Rights Department has issued guidance on algorithmic discrimination, and new AI bills are expected in 2025-2026. Employers using AI tools in hiring or HR decisions should conduct bias audits proactively.
compliance_actions: Inventory all AI tools used in hiring, performance management, or termination decisions
Conduct bias audits on AI/algorithmic tools per DFEH guidance
Review vendor contracts for AI tools — ensure they indemnify for discrimination claims
Monitor California legislature for new AI employment bills in 2025-2026
